Started about a week ago. Nobody wants to go near it after sundown.
The sound comes and goes. Always at the same hour.
Whatever it is, it's getting more frequent.
First it was just the lights. Then the ground started trembling.
We thought it was thunder at first. But the sky was clear every time.
Three nights running, the well water turned brackish. By morning it's fine again.
The old stones started humming. You can feel it through your boots if you stand close enough.
Everyone who's gone to investigate comes back quiet. Not hurt, just... reluctant to talk about it.
It always happens between second and third watch. Like something knows when the guard changes.
The effect spreads outward. You can see it in the ground, the water, the air.
It's been getting worse. What started as odd has become hard to ignore.
People who've seen it up close don't want to talk about it.
The damage is visible if you know where to look.
The area around it has gone quiet. Unnaturally so.
Fog rolls in from the east now. Used to come from the river. Small thing, but it's wrong.
We set up lanterns along the south path. Every morning, they're snuffed out. Wicks aren't burned down, just out.
Even the children have noticed. That's what worries me most.
Livestock won't drink from the lower stream anymore. They'd rather go thirsty.
Two of the elders say they've been dreaming the same dream. Won't say what it's about.
Someone or something is leaving marks. Deliberate ones.
Things that shouldn't move have been moving on their own.
It's affecting tools and instruments now. Things that should be reliable aren't.
The shadows fall strange near the crossroads. Longer than they should be, even at noon.
A sinkhole opened behind the storehouse. Perfectly round, like something pushed up from underneath.
It started small. A bad feeling. Now half the settlement is sleeping with a blade under the pillow.
The pattern repeats every three days. We've charted it. Whatever causes it keeps a schedule.
Crops in the eastern field wilted overnight. Not drought, not disease. Just dead, all at once.
A pillar of dust rose up from the flats with no wind to carry it. Stood there for an hour, then collapsed.
The fire pit wouldn't light for three days. Same wood, same tinder. On the fourth day, it caught like normal.
Birds left the area first. Then the insects. By the third day, the silence was deafening.
The stones in the foundation cracked. Not from settling: the fractures radiate outward from a single point.
Someone found a circle of scorched earth in the clearing. Perfect circumference, grass burned to ash inside, untouched outside.
The old sundial started reading wrong. An hour off, consistently. Nobody's moved it.
Metal objects near the site are magnetized. Buckles stick to each other. Nails pull toward the east wall.
A section of the path sank by a handspan overnight. The ground beneath is hollow now, where it wasn't before.
The stars looked wrong for two nights. Not missing, just shifted, like the sky rotated a few degrees.
Water froze in the troughs on a warm night. Not a cold snap: the air was comfortable. Just the water.
Every dog in the settlement howled at the same time. Lasted about a minute, then they all stopped at once.
Tree rings on a freshly cut stump showed a gap. A year with no growth at all. The forester says that's impossible.
A whole stand of trees dropped their leaves in a single afternoon. Out of season by months.
The echo near the gorge changed. Used to bounce back once. Now it comes back twice, with a delay that doesn't match the geometry.
Rain fell in one specific area. Clear sky, no clouds, but a steady drizzle in a circle about twenty paces across.
The ground was warm to the touch in a patch behind the smithy. Not from the forge: the heat came from below.
Static built up in everything. Touching a door handle threw sparks. Wool clothing crackled all day.
A flock of birds flew in a perfect grid pattern. Evenly spaced, uniform altitude. Birds don't do that.
The moon looked red for three nights. Not hazy or dim: saturated red, like it was reflecting a fire we couldn't see.
Sound carried strangely. You could hear conversations from across the settlement as if the speaker were beside you.
The creek reversed flow for about an hour. Backed up from downstream, filled the banks, then returned to normal.
Compass needles spin near the site. Not wandering: spinning, steadily, like something is pulling them in circles.
The settlement bell rang on its own. No wind, no one near the tower. A single clear tone, then nothing.
New springs appeared on the hillside. Three of them, in a line, flowing water that tastes of copper.
The timber in the old hall started weeping sap. Wood that's been dried and cured for decades, suddenly wet.
Lightning struck the same tree four times in one storm. Not nearby: the same branch, the same scar.
A patch of wildflowers bloomed out of season. Dead of winter, frozen ground, and there they were.
Stones in the wall rearranged themselves. The mason swears the pattern was different that morning.
Footprints appeared in the frost on the inside of the windows. Barefoot, child-sized, but no children had been in the room.
The temperature drops sharply within a specific boundary. Step inside it and your breath fogs. Step out and it's warm.
Every candle in the hall guttered at the same instant. Didn't go out: just dipped, then came back.
A sound like a massive exhalation came from underground. Felt the ground shift, a warm breeze from a crack in the rock.
The fish in the pond surfaced all at once. Floated belly-up for an hour, then righted themselves and swam normally.
Dew forms on the stones there even at midday. Heavy, dripping dew, as if the stone itself sweats.
The wind dies completely within fifty paces of the site. Absolute stillness, even when a gale blows elsewhere.
Smoke from the evening fires drifted downward instead of up. Pooled on the ground like fog. Lasted until dawn.
The grain in the storehouse germinated overnight. Still in the sacks, bone dry, but every kernel had sprouted.
Shadows of objects appeared on the wall with no light source to cast them. Fixed in place, dark and sharp.
The weathervane spins continuously. Not with the wind: against it, at a constant speed.
An old well that's been dry for years started producing water again. Cold, clear, but with a faint blue tint.
The settlement dogs dug in the same spot simultaneously. Four of them, different owners, all converging on one point in the yard.
A sound like hammer strikes echoes from the old mine at regular intervals. The mine has been sealed for a generation.
Bread wouldn't rise for a week. Every baker in the settlement had the same problem. Then it stopped.
The wooden fence posts along the south boundary are warm to the touch. Not from sun exposure: they're warm at night too.
People report a recurring taste of iron in their mouths. No cause found. Comes and goes without pattern.
A perfectly straight line of dead grass appeared overnight. Runs from the well to the edge of the forest. Ruler-straight.
The rooftops were coated in a fine ash one morning. No fire, no eruption anywhere nearby. Just ash, evenly distributed.
The clock in the council hall runs backward for exactly one hour each night. Then corrects itself.
A humming sound emanates from a specific stone in the wall. Constant pitch, constant volume. Touching the stone stops it temporarily.
The air above the pond shimmers like a heat mirage, even on cold mornings. No warmth beneath it. Just the visual distortion.
Tree sap flows upward on one particular trunk. Defies everything the foresters know. They've been watching it for days.
Every mirror in the building showed a slight delay. Reflections moved a half-second after the subject. Then it stopped.
Seeds planted near the site grow at triple the normal rate. The plants look healthy but they taste of nothing.
A low vibration hums through the bedrock. You can't hear it above ground, but cellars and basements amplify it.
The aurora was visible for three consecutive nights. We're too far south for that. The elders have no explanation.
Rust appeared on iron tools stored indoors overnight. Sealed, dry storage. Everything touched by it pitted and flaked.
A thin layer of frost covered the interior walls of one house. Only one. The houses on either side were unaffected.
Pottery cracked on shelves simultaneously. Every piece in the pantry, at the same moment. Nothing else was disturbed.
An underground stream shifted course. Wells that were reliable for decades went dry. New ones produce where none did before.
Insects swarm at the boundary line. Not crossing it: massing at the edge, as if drawn by something just beyond.
Stone dust sifted down from the ceiling for hours. No cracks, no visible damage above. Just a slow, steady fall of powder.
The bonfire threw no sparks that night. Burned clean and vertical, perfectly contained. Every other fire we've lit has been normal.
Birds circle the area but won't land. They orbit in a wide loop, then leave. We've watched them do it dozens of times.
The path to the lower field felt longer. Measured it: same distance. But the walk takes a quarter-hour more than it used to.
All the flowers in the meadow turned to face the same direction. Not toward the sun: toward the stone circle.
A smell of petrichor persists near the site. Rain smell, constant, even during a drought. The ground there is dry.
Clothes left on the line overnight were found neatly folded on the ground beneath. No wind, no one awake.
The thatch on one roof bleached white overnight. Straw that was golden yesterday is now bone-pale.
A resonant tone fills the gorge at dawn. Lasts about ten minutes, rises in pitch, then cuts off abruptly.
The soil in one field has turned to powder. Not dried out: powdered, like it was ground between stones. Can't plant in it.
A boulder in the field has moved three paces from where it sat for a generation. No tracks, no tool marks. Just moved.
Fruit ripened overnight. Entire orchards, all at once. Over-ripe by morning. Fell from the branches in soft piles.
The tanning vats curdled. Every one, same night. The tanners say the chemistry is impossible without contamination.
Worms surfaced across the whole lower meadow. Thousands of them, writhing on the grass at midnight. Gone by dawn.
The wind carries words. Not clearly: fragments, syllables, as if someone far away were speaking and the sound is arriving out of order.
A spiral pattern appeared pressed into the field. Not trampled: pressed, as if by steady even weight from above.
The moss on the north wall dried and fell off in a single day. The stone beneath is scored with fine lines.
Smoke rises from the lake surface at dawn. Not steam: actual smoke, grey and acrid. The water is cold.
An entire flock of sheep lay down at noon and refused to move until the following morning. Healthy, just... still.
The color of the sunset has been wrong for a week. Too orange, too long. The light lingers past when it should be dark.
A tree split down the center overnight. Clean split, right down the heartwood. No storm, no lightning.
The local bees abandoned their hives simultaneously. Every colony. They haven't returned.
Night sounds vanished. No crickets, no frogs, no owls. Complete biological silence from dusk until dawn.
The stone floor in the old hall developed condensation in the shape of footprints. Appearing and fading in sequence, like something walking.
Rope left near the site unraveled overnight. Not frayed: systematically unwound, fiber by fiber.
A column of warm air rises from one spot in the clearing. Steady, about body temperature, carrying a faint mineral smell.
The gate swings open at the same time each night. Latched, barred, doesn't matter. At third watch, it opens.
Teeth marks appeared in timber beams. Not on the outside: on structural members sealed inside the walls.
The apple trees flowered twice in one season. The second bloom was pale and the petals fell as dust.
A deep thudding sound comes from the ridge. Irregular, like a massive heartbeat. Lasts a few minutes, then stops for hours.
Every container of water left uncovered develops a thin film by morning. Iridescent, oily. Skimming it off makes no difference by the next day.
Chalk lines appeared on the floor of the assembly hall. Geometric patterns, precise, no dust or tools nearby.
The oxen refused to pull toward the east. Would work any other direction without complaint. Toward the east, they planted their hooves and would not budge.
A smell of burning filled the settlement for one afternoon. No source found. Checked every hearth, every forge, every oven.
Dusk lasted three hours that evening. The sun sat on the horizon and would not set. Then it dropped all at once.
The bark on every birch tree in the grove peeled away simultaneously. Curled off in long strips and fell.
Someone's handprint appeared on the inside of a sealed window. Pressed flat, fingers splayed. On the side facing the room.
The rats left. Not driven out: they filed out in an orderly line, crossed the yard, and disappeared into the drainage ditch.
A patch of air near the old well refracts light. You can see through it, but everything on the other side is slightly displaced.
The hay bales in storage generated enough heat to steam. No combustion: just warmth, rising steadily for two days.
Cracks in the dry ground formed a radial pattern overnight. All emanating from a single point, equidistant.
The flag on the council hall wrapped itself around the pole so tightly it tore. No wind at the time.
Standing water in puddles and troughs vibrated for an hour. Fine ripples, concentric, as if something deep below was drumming.
A patch of lichen grew across the entire south wall of the granary overnight. Thick, grey, covering the stone completely.
The forge fire burned blue for a day. Temperature was the same, the bellows were the same. Just the color changed.
Every door in the inn swelled shut on the same morning. Humidity was normal. The carpenter couldn't explain it.
An impression appeared in the soil. Like a body had lain there, but pressed an inch deep into packed earth.
The river brought down objects that don't belong upstream. A chair leg. A clay pot. A child's shoe. All from different eras.
Spiderwebs in the storehouse aligned geometrically. Parallel strands, equal spacing. Not natural construction.
The sunrise was silent. No birdsong, no rooster, no wind. Total silence until the sun cleared the ridge, then everything started at once.
Stones stacked themselves on the riverbank. Small ones, balanced carefully. The fisher swears nobody was there.
The temperature underground inverted. Cellars were warmer than the surface for a full day. Then it corrected.
A vibration in the floorboards kept time. Regular as a pendulum, felt through the soles of your feet.
The pond water turned opaque overnight. Not muddy: milky white, like chalk dissolved in it. It cleared after three days.
All the cats gathered at the edge of the settlement and stared into the dark for an hour. Then dispersed without incident.
A wall developed a damp patch in the exact shape of a hand. Dried by afternoon. Returned the next morning in the same spot.
The grass in the courtyard grew a foot overnight. Lush, thick, dark green. Then it died the next day and turned to straw.
An acrid, chemical smell drifted from the old mine shaft. The mine has been dry and sealed for twenty years.
Chalk cliffs along the shore crumbled in a line, as if something had drawn a finger along them at water level.
Every lock in the building jammed simultaneously. Not rusted: seized, as if the mechanisms fused. Freed themselves by morning.
A circle of mushrooms appeared overnight. Perfect ring, three paces across. The variety doesn't grow in this region.
Condensation on the windows formed patterns. Not random: repeated motifs, spirals and angles, the same on every pane.
The settlement's weathervane pointed due north for a week. Through storms, through calm. Fixed in place, then freed itself.
A low moan came from the walls during the coldest hour. Not wind: the stone itself seemed to contract and groan.
Leaves blew in a perfect vortex in the center of the square. Tight, contained, spinning for half an hour with no wind.
Every piece of charcoal in the forge pit reignited at once. Cold pit, no tinder, no spark. Just sudden, uniform combustion.
A root pushed through the floor of the cellar. In one night, it crossed the room. The tree it connects to is fifty paces away.
The morning dew was warm. Not cool: body-temperature warm. Only in the meadow. Everywhere else, normal.
A set of tracks appeared in fresh snowfall that led to the center of the field and stopped. No return tracks, no disturbance.
Mushrooms grew from the wooden tabletop in the council room overnight. Living wood sprouts: the table hasn't been living wood for years.
The waterfall upstream went silent for one full day. Still flowing, same volume. Just no sound.
Something turned every signpost in the settlement to face the same direction. All of them, overnight.
The air pressure dropped so fast that sealed jars popped their lids. Then normalized within minutes.
Every piece of leather in the tannery softened overnight. Cured hides went back to rawhide. Weeks of work, undone.
A line of salt appeared across the threshold of the main hall. Fine grain, evenly spread. Nobody takes credit for placing it.
The clay in the riverbank hardened to something near ceramic. Same clay that's been soft mud for as long as anyone remembers.
Small pebbles accumulated on windowsills overnight. Not thrown: placed, in neat rows, sorted by size.
An afterimage of the sun persisted in the sky for an hour past sunset. Like it left a stain on the air.
The well chain lowered itself to full length overnight. Every link of slack paid out. Whoever wound it back up found no resistance.
Every flame in the settlement leaned the same direction for an evening. Not toward wind: toward the eastern ridge. Steady, unflickering.
A sound like tearing fabric came from the sky on a clear day. Loud, sustained, then gone. Nothing visible.
The mortar between stones in the old wall turned to powder overnight. The stones held by friction alone. A single push would collapse it.
Dust motes in a shaft of light moved against the air current. Streaming upward in a column, defying every draft.
A smell of fresh-turned earth filled the settlement, though no one had been digging. Strongest near the center, fading at the edges.
The fish traps downstream were full of a species nobody recognized. Pale, eyeless, from deep water. The river shouldn't have those.
A sound like distant singing drifted from the caves. No words, just melody. Faint, but multiple people heard it on the same night.
The color drained from flowers near the site. Not wilting: still alive and upright, just bleached white from root to petal.
Iron filings arranged themselves into lines on the workbench overnight. The smith hadn't touched them. Perfect parallel rows.
A tremor hit, but only the buildings on one side of the road felt it. The other side was perfectly still.
Milk curdled in every household simultaneously. Same morning, same hour. Fresh milk, properly stored.
The horizon shimmered all day. Not heat haze: a rippling, as if the air itself were flexing.
A perfect sphere of clear ice formed in the middle of the pond. Warm day, unfrozen water all around it.
The soot in the chimney rearranged into a pattern. The sweep refused to describe what he saw.
A whispering sound follows anyone who walks past the old marker stone. Stops when you stop. Resumes when you move.
Someone noticed the constellation had an extra star. Faint, but visible. It wasn't there the previous season.
Puddles reflected a sky that didn't match the one above. Different cloud patterns, different light.
The bees produced dark honey for a week. Nearly black, with a bitter edge. They've gone back to normal.
Clocks and hourglasses throughout the settlement disagreed by exactly the same margin. All fast by the same amount.
A tree stump that was cut years ago put out a single green shoot. Healthy, vigorous. The stump had been dead.
The torches in the lower passage extinguished from one end to the other, in sequence, as if something passed through.
Paw prints appeared on the roof tiles. Heavy ones. Nothing should be walking on rooftops.
A stone that's been a boundary marker for decades was found twenty paces from its original position. No one moved it. No one could: it weighs as much as an ox.
The grain sacks in the storehouse rearranged themselves into a line. Every sack, oriented the same direction. The storehouse keeper swears the door was barred.
A hairline crack ran the full length of the stone bridge overnight. Straight, clean, following no fault line. The mason says the stone is sound everywhere except that one line.

The crows gathered on the council roof and would not leave for three days. Hundreds of them, silent. When they finally left, there was not a single dropping. As if they had been waiting, not roosting.
A bucket left by the well was found crushed flat. Not stepped on: compressed evenly from all sides, as if squeezed by an invisible hand.
The elder's walking stick, hung on a hook by the door every night for twenty years, was found standing upright in the center of the room. Balanced on its tip.
The grain mash in the brewery fermented in reverse. The alcohol content dropped to nothing overnight. The brewer says that is chemically impossible.
A stone in the courtyard was hot enough to boil water poured on it. The stones beside it were cold. By evening, the heat had moved to a different stone.
Every plumb line in the settlement pointed slightly east of true. Not enough to notice casually, but the builders caught it when the walls started going up crooked.
Dried flowers hanging in the rafters rehydrated overnight. Full color, flexible stems. They had been dead for two years.
The footpath between the inn and the market developed a slight incline that had not been there before. Downhill going north, where it used to be level. The stones have not moved.
Ink faded from every document stored in the records hall. Not all at once: gradually, over three days, until the pages were blank. The ink in sealed bottles was unaffected.
An iron fence post bent at a right angle overnight. No force marks, no heat marks. The metal simply curved as if it had been shaped in a forge.
Cobwebs appeared in rooms that had been swept clean the evening before. Not a few strands: dense, layered webs, as if the spiders had been working for weeks.
The fire in the main hearth changed shape. It burned in a column, narrow and tall, instead of spreading across the logs. Steady, contained, for about an hour.
A stone dropped from the wall of the watchtower fell upward. Struck the parapet above, chipped it, and came to rest on the walkway. Three guards witnessed it.
The lantern oil in every lamp turned viscous overnight. Thick, sluggish, barely wicking. By noon it was normal again.
The wind carried a warmth from the north for an entire day. North wind is always cold here. This was warm, dry, and carried a scent like hot sand.
A wooden beam in the granary sprouted buds. Sawn, dried, installed timber, pushing out new growth. The carpenter cut them off and they returned the next morning.
Rainwater collected in a basin formed a pattern of concentric rings. Not from dripping: the surface arranged itself into circles while sitting still.
The well rope was found braided. Three strands where there had been one. The same total length. Nobody had touched it.
A patch of frost formed in the shape of a leaf on the inside of the window. Not a real leaf pressed against the glass: a crystalline replica, symmetrical and detailed.
The flagstones in the central path rearranged their color pattern overnight. The alternating grey and brown sequence reversed. Same stones, different order.
Every wooden door in the settlement swelled by exactly the same amount on the same night. Not warping: uniform expansion, as if the wood had absorbed moisture that was not in the air.
A mound of earth appeared in the center of a field that had been flat and plowed the day before. Knee-high, roughly conical, packed firm. No footprints around it.
The smoke from the forge spiraled counterclockwise for an entire afternoon. It always rises straight or drifts with the wind. It has never spiraled before.
A pile of tools left in the yard was found arranged by size, smallest to largest, in a neat row. The owner left them in a heap.
The water in the horse trough developed a surface tension strong enough to support a nail. The smith tested it himself and could not explain the result.
A sound like a single heavy footstep shook the floorboards of the inn at midnight. No second step. Just one, then silence.
The flour in the bakery was warm to the touch. Not heated: warm, as if it had been sitting in sun. The bakery has no windows.
Glass bottles on the shelf hummed a chord for about thirty seconds. All of them, simultaneously. Different pitches based on how full they were. Then silence.
A fence section was found rotated ninety degrees. Still upright, still intact, just facing a different direction. The post holes matched the new orientation.
The path to the old shrine felt downhill in both directions. Walking there, downhill. Walking back, downhill. Nobody else found this remarkable, which was itself remarkable.
Bark peeled from trees in the grove and drifted toward a central point, like leaves in a current. Piled there in a rough mound. The trees showed no other damage.
Every quill in the scribe's desk snapped at the same point. Midway along the shaft, clean breaks. She had a dozen prepared. All broke at first use.
The sundial's shadow split into two. Both shadows pointed in slightly different directions, as if cast by two suns. Lasted through the afternoon, then merged at dusk.
A puddle in the courtyard reflected the night sky during the day. Stars, moon, darkness. Everything else reflected normally. Just that one puddle.
The sawdust on the workshop floor arranged itself into concentric circles overnight. The carpenter swept it into a pile before leaving. The circles were there when he returned.
A copper pot left on the shelf developed a patina in the shape of a handprint. Green oxidation, clearly defined fingers and palm. The pot had not been touched in weeks.
The thatch on the eastern cottages dried out and turned brittle in a single afternoon. The western cottages, same material and age, were unaffected. The dividing line followed the road precisely.
Every nail in the floorboards of the inn loosened by exactly the same amount. Not enough to pull free: enough to creak where there had been silence.
A stone trough cracked along a line that spelled something. The mason insists it is a natural stress fracture. The letters, if they are letters, are in no language anyone reads.
The creek water turned sweet for one day. Not contaminated: genuinely sweet, like honey had been dissolved in it. The source was never identified and the sweetness did not return.
A leather belt hanging on a hook in the tannery tied itself into a knot overnight. A proper knot: a bowline. The tanner says nobody entered the building.
The chimney smoke from every house in the settlement rose to the same height and stopped, forming a flat layer. As if the air above that altitude were solid.
A wooden post driven into the ground last season has grown roots. Living roots, extending into the soil, drawing moisture. The post is seasoned timber and should not be capable of this.
Every egg collected that morning had a double yolk. Every hen, every nest. The farmer has been keeping poultry for thirty years and says the odds are incalculable.
A stretch of wall plaster cracked in a pattern that matches the constellation visible above the settlement at midnight. The plaster was applied six months ago. The pattern appeared in a single night.
The beeswax candles in the chapel melted into identical shapes. Not pooling: forming. Small spirals, uniform, as if poured into molds. Every candle, the same result.
A pile of firewood restacked itself. The owner split and stacked it haphazardly before dark. By morning it was arranged by length and diameter. She lives alone.
The well water developed an echo. Drop a stone and the splash returns twice: once from the water, once from somewhere below the water. The second echo is deeper.
The morning frost covered every surface except the footpath. A perfect corridor of unfrosted ground, as if something warm had walked the path before dawn.
A clay jug on the potter's shelf cracked and resealed itself three times in a week. Each time the crack followed a different path. Each time it closed without intervention.
The wooden bridge creaked in a rhythm that the carpenter recognized as a tune. A lullaby, specifically. He did not find this reassuring.
Every piece of fruit in the storehouse ripened simultaneously, regardless of when it was picked. Apples stored a month ago and pears stored a day ago reached the same state at the same hour.
The watch fire at the north gate burned without consuming fuel for an entire night. The logs were the same size at dawn as they had been at dusk. The fire was hot, the coals were real.
A ring of dead insects appeared around the base of the old oak. Perfect circle, maybe two paces across. Moths, beetles, flies. Different species, all dead, none decayed.
The cellar stairs felt wrong underfoot for a day. Not uneven: soft, as if the stone had the give of packed earth. By evening they were solid again and the mason found no structural change.
A cooking pot left hanging over a dead fire was found boiling the next morning. The fire was cold ash. The water was at a full rolling boil. It stopped the moment someone lifted the lid.

The hay in the loft rearranged itself into a shape. Not a pile: a deliberate form, like a figure lying on its side. The farmhand dismantled it and by the next morning it had re-formed in the same position.

A single note sang from the rooftop at dawn, clear and sustained, as if someone were holding a tuning fork against the ridge beam. It lasted thirty seconds and did not repeat. The roofer found nothing unusual.

Grain scattered for the chickens rolled uphill toward the coop door. Not blown: rolling, individually, against the slope. The chickens watched it happen and refused to eat from that patch for the rest of the day.
It started not long after someone went poking around {subject_focus_the}.
The disturbance seems to radiate from {subject_focus_the}. Strongest at the center, weaker further out.
Whatever is happening at {subject_focus_the} follows a cycle. Regular intervals, almost predictable.
The effects near {subject_focus_the} are getting stronger. What was subtle a week ago is now impossible to ignore.
People who investigate {subject_focus_the} come back unsettled. Not hurt, but changed somehow.
The damage near {subject_focus_the} is localized. A circle of effect with clear boundaries.
The disturbance at {subject_focus_the} is worse at night. During the day it is almost unnoticeable.
Attempts to seal off {subject_focus_the} have not stopped the effects from spreading.
The area around {subject_focus_the} has developed its own weather pattern. Fog, even on clear days.
The phenomenon at {subject_focus_the} has been active for weeks now with no sign of stopping.
A scholar examined {subject_focus_the} and said the event is not natural. The mechanism is unknown.
The intensity of the disruption at {subject_focus_the} fluctuates. Peaks and troughs, like a tide.
Whatever is happening at {subject_focus_the}, it began suddenly. One day nothing, the next day this.
The ground near {subject_focus_the} has shifted visibly since the disturbance began.
Readings taken near {subject_focus_the} are inconsistent. The same measurement gives different results each time.
People within a certain distance of {subject_focus_the} report headaches. Beyond that line, nothing.
The air near {subject_focus_the} has a charge to it. Like the moment before a lightning strike.
Whatever triggered the events at {subject_focus_the} may still be active. The effects have not diminished.
A diviner tried to assess {subject_focus_the} and described the energy there as old. Very old.
The event at {subject_focus_the} seems to have awakened something that was dormant. How long, nobody knows.
Water drawn from near {subject_focus_the} leaves a residue that evaporates but returns.
The tremors centered on {subject_focus_the} are too regular to be natural. Spaced evenly, same duration.
Birds that fly over {subject_focus_the} veer away at the same point. A consistent boundary.
The disruption at {subject_focus_the} has a sound component. Low frequency. You feel it more than hear it.
Investigators at {subject_focus_the} reported that their equipment behaved erratically. Tools misfired, readings wandered.
The event at {subject_focus_the} correlates with lunar phases. Strongest at the full moon.
The pattern at {subject_focus_the} suggests something building. Each cycle slightly stronger than the last.
Soil samples from {subject_focus_the} show chemical changes that should take years, compressed into weeks.
The boundary of the effect at {subject_focus_the} is expanding. Slowly, but measurably.
Whatever is happening at {subject_focus_the} has a source. Finding it is the problem.
